title: Glechoma subincisa
taxonomy: Glechoma subincisa is classified in the family Lamiaceae, commonly known as the mint family. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics, such as its square stem and opposite or whorled leaves. The genus Glechoma is a group of flowering plants that are native to Asia. The species subincisa is native to China and Korea. Glechoma subincisa is also known as Korean mint or creeping glechoma.
uses: Glechoma subincisa, also known as Chinese artichoke, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its leaves and stems are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, making it useful for treating wounds and skin conditions. In traditional Chinese medicine, the plant is used to treat digestive issues, such as bloating and indigestion, due to its purported ability to regulate the flow of qi. Additionally, it is sometimes used as a natural remedy for fever reduction and to boost the immune system. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Glechoma subincisa for medicinal purposes.

constituents: Glechoma subincisa, also known as Korean mint, contains a variety of active constituents, including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids. intro: Glechoma subincisa, commonly known as Japanese mint or purple mint, is a perennial herb native to East Asia, belonging to the mint family (Lamiaceae). It is valued for its medicinal properties, which include antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities, contributing to its potential health benefits, such as reducing stress and improving digestion. The bioactive constituents of Glechoma subincisa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and essential oils, particularly menthone, which may be responsible for its pharmacological effects. In traditional herbal medicine, Glechoma subincisa is commonly used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as being used in topical applications, such as creams and ointments, to alleviate skin issues and promote wound healing.
